basicWordCounting.name: Basic cases
basicWordCounting.description: Write a countWords function that takes a sentence and returns a dictionary with each word as a key and its frequency as the value. Handle spaces, commas, punctuation, and numbers.

caseNormalization.name: Case normalization
caseNormalization.description: Ensure your function handles mixed case words correctly, treats apostrophes as part of words, and handles multiple spaces.

bonusApostrophes.name: Bonus: Apostrophes and quotations
bonusApostrophes.description: Handle edge cases where apostrophes are used as quotation marks around words, not as part of contractions. Only apostrophes in the middle of a word should be kept.

wordCountMultipleUniqueWords.name: Count one of each word
wordCountMultipleUniqueWords.description: Count one of each word in a sentence.

wordCountMultipleOccurrences.name: Multiple occurrences
wordCountMultipleOccurrences.description: Count multiple occurrences of a word.

wordCountWithApostrophesAndQuotations.name: Words with apostrophes and quotations
wordCountWithApostrophesAndQuotations.description: Handle words with apostrophes and quotations.

wordCountApostropheWordVariation.name: Apostrophe word variation
wordCountApostropheWordVariation.description: Correctly count variations of words with apostrophes.

caseInsensitive.question: How do I make the counting case-insensitive?
caseInsensitive.answer: Lowercase the whole sentence once at the start. Then 'The' and 'the' both become 'the' and your dictionary will count them together.

splitWords.question: How do I split the sentence into words?
splitWords.answer: Loop through each character. If it's a letter, digit, or apostrophe, it's part of the current word, so append it. Otherwise (space, punctuation), the word is finished, so push it to your list of words and start fresh.

countEachWord.question: How do I count each word?
countEachWord.answer: Use a dictionary keyed by the word. For each word, check whether it already exists as a key. If yes, add 1 to its count. If no, set it to 1.

checkKeyExists.question: How do I check if a key already exists?
checkKeyExists.answer: Use `hasKey(dict, word)`. It returns true if that word is already in the dictionary.

toLowerCase.description: Convert a string to lowercase (provided by level stdlib)

push.description: Add an element to a list and return the new list (provided by level stdlib)

hasKey.description: Check if a key exists in a dictionary, returns true or false (provided by level stdlib)
